section.title: Paul's Rights as an Apostle
section.sectionRationale: Having told the strong to lay down their freedom for the sake of a weaker brother, Paul now offers himself as the living proof. He is a free man and a genuine apostle with every right to material support and to a believing wife, yet he refuses to use those rights so that nothing will hinder the gospel. The chapter turns the abstract argument of chapter eight into a portrait: a man who gladly surrenders what is his to win others to Christ, and who disciplines himself like an athlete so that after preaching to others he will not be disqualified.
section.thesis: A true apostle with real rights lays them down for the gospel, becoming a slave to all and all things to all people so that by all possible means he might save some, and he runs and trains with strict discipline for an imperishable crown, lest after preaching to others he himself be disqualified.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Paul the free apostle and his rights; the many witnesses that gospel workers deserve support; the rights he surrenders because he is compelled to preach; and the great turn, becoming all things to all people and running to win the imperishable crown.

verses.text: Am I not free? Am I not an apostle? Have I not seen Jesus our Lord? Are you yourselves not my workmanship in the Lord?
Even if I am not an apostle to others, surely I am to you. For you are the seal of my apostleship in the Lord.
This is my defense to those who scrutinize me:
Have we no right to food and to drink?
Have we no right to take along a believing wife, as do the other apostles and the Lord’s brothers and Cephas?
Or are Barnabas and I the only apostles who must work for a living?
Who serves as a soldier at his own expense? Who plants a vineyard and does not eat of its fruit? Who tends a flock and does not drink of its milk?
Do I say this from a human perspective? Doesn’t the Law say the same thing?
For it is written in the Law of Moses: “Do not muzzle an ox while it is treading out the grain.” Is it about oxen that God is concerned?
Isn’t He actually speaking on our behalf? Indeed, this was written for us, because when the plowman plows and the thresher threshes, they should also expect to share in the harvest.
If we have sown spiritual seed among you, is it too much for us to reap a material harvest from you?
If others have this {{exousia|right}} to your support, shouldn’t we have it all the more? But we did not exercise this right. Instead, we put up with anything rather than hinder the gospel of Christ.
Do you not know that those who work in the temple eat of its food, and those who serve at the altar partake of its offerings?
In the same way, the Lord has prescribed that those who preach the gospel should receive their living from the {{euangelion|gospel}}.
But I have not used any of these rights. And I am not writing this to suggest that something be done for me. Indeed, I would rather die than let anyone nullify my boast.
Yet when I preach the gospel, I have no reason to boast, because I am obligated to preach. Woe to me if I do not preach the gospel!
If my preaching is voluntary, I have a reward. But if it is not voluntary, I am still entrusted with a responsibility.
What then is my reward? That in preaching the gospel I may offer it free of charge, and so not use up my rights in preaching it.

units.label: All things to all, run to win

verses.text: Though I am free of obligation to anyone, I make myself {{douloo|a slave}} to everyone, to win as many as possible.
To the Jews I became like a Jew, to win the Jews. To those under the law I became like one under the law (though I myself am not under the law), to win those under the law.
To those without the law I became like one without the law (though I am not outside the law of God but am under the law of Christ), to win those without the law.
To the weak I became weak, to win the weak. I have become all things to all people so that by all possible means I might save some.
I do all this for the sake of the gospel, so that I may share in its blessings.
Do you not know that in a race all the runners run, but only one receives the prize? Run in such a way as to take the prize.
Everyone who competes in the games trains with strict discipline. They do it for a crown that is perishable, but we do it for a {{stephanos|crown}} that is imperishable.
Therefore I do not run aimlessly; I do not fight like I am beating the air.
No, I discipline my body and make it my slave, so that after I have preached to others, I myself will not be {{adokimos|disqualified}}.

exousia.gloss: Right, authority, freedom to act. Paul insists he holds a real exousia to material support, the same right he grants to others, yet he chooses not to use it. The word matters to the whole argument: freedom is genuine only if you are truly free to lay it down for love's sake.

euangelion.gloss: Gospel, good news, the announcement of what God has done in Christ crucified and risen. It is the hinge of the chapter: the Lord Himself has arranged that gospel workers live from the gospel, yet the same gospel is so precious to Paul that he waives his living rather than put any obstacle in its way.

douloo.gloss: To enslave, to make a slave. Though free from all, Paul enslaves himself to everyone to win as many as possible. It is a deliberate echo of Christ, who took the form of a servant; freedom in Christ expresses itself not in self-assertion but in glad self-lowering for the good of others.

stephanos.gloss: A victor's wreath or crown, the garland awarded to the winner of the games. The athlete disciplines himself for a wreath that withers; the believer runs for a crown that never fades. It names the imperishable reward of those who finish faithfully in Christ.

adokimos.gloss: Failing the test, rejected, disqualified. Paul fears that a preacher who does not discipline himself could, like an athlete breaking the rules, be ruled out. It is a sober warning that gifting and preaching are no substitute for a life of persevering, self-controlled faith.

basic.context: Paul has just told the Corinthians who have knowledge to give up their freedom to eat idol food for the sake of a weaker brother. Now he shows them how it is done, using his own life. He really is a free man and a real apostle, with every right to be paid for his work and to travel with a wife, as Peter and the other apostles did. Yet he refused to take a penny from the Corinthians, working with his own hands instead, so that no one could ever accuse him of preaching for money and so hinder the gospel. He made himself a servant of everyone, adapting to Jew and Gentile, weak and strong, to win as many as possible. And he compares the Christian life to an athlete in training, running to win a crown that will never fade.
basic.hermeneutics: This chapter is a worked example, not a change of subject. Paul is defending his apostleship, but chiefly he is modelling the very love he asked for in chapter eight: rights freely surrendered so that others may be saved. The right to support is real and Christ commanded it, yet love can lay a real right down. Read verse 27 rightly: it is a call to persevere and discipline oneself, not a claim that a believer can lose salvation by a single slip.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul holds a real exousia, a right, to material support, yet will not use it. He waives his living for the sake of the euangelion, the gospel, which he must preach. Though free from all, he uses the strong word douloo, to enslave himself, to everyone to win them. And he runs like an athlete for a stephanos, a victor's crown, that is imperishable, disciplining himself lest he be adokimos, disqualified.
basic.typology: The temple and the altar point forward: as the priests of Israel lived from the offerings, so gospel ministers live from the gospel, the shadow fulfilled in the new covenant ministry. The athlete's crown, a fading wreath in the games, foreshadows the imperishable crown Christ gives to those who finish the race by faith.
basic.christ: Behind Paul's self-surrender stands Christ Himself, who though rich became poor, who took the form of a servant and made Himself nothing to win us. When Paul enslaves himself to all to save some, he is imitating his Lord. The gospel he refuses to hinder is the message of Christ crucified, and the imperishable crown he runs for is given by Christ, the righteous Judge.
basic.god: God is the one who prescribed that those who preach the gospel should live from it, and God is the one who compels Paul with a holy necessity, woe to me if I do not preach. He rewards faithful labour and He is the searcher who will test each servant's work. He calls His people to a disciplined, persevering life, and He supplies the grace to run.
basic.crossReferences: In the Gospels Jesus says the worker is worthy of his wages. Philippians tells of the Son who emptied Himself and took the form of a servant. Second Timothy speaks of finishing the race and the crown of righteousness. Second Corinthians shows Paul refusing support so as not to burden the churches.
basic.oneStory: The whole Bible moves toward a Saviour who lays down His rights to win a people, and toward a people who, filled with His Spirit, do the same. Paul's surrendered rights and disciplined running are a small picture of the gospel itself: freedom used not for self but for the salvation of others, until the race is finished and the imperishable crown is given.

context.literary: Chapter nine is a sustained self-defense that functions as an illustration within the larger argument of chapters eight to ten about food offered to idols and Christian freedom. It opens with a volley of rhetorical questions establishing Paul's apostleship and rights, builds through examples from soldiering, farming, the Law, and the temple that a worker deserves support, pivots on his refusal to use that right, and climaxes in the imagery of the runner and the boxer straining for the prize.
context.historical: In Greco-Roman culture travelling teachers took fees and patronage, and some at Corinth apparently judged Paul's manual labour and refusal of pay as proof that he was a second-rate apostle. Corinth also hosted the Isthmian Games, so the athletic imagery of the closing verses would have struck home vividly; every reader knew the runner's discipline and the victor's perishable wreath of withering leaves.
context.canonical: The chapter joins the New Testament's teaching that gospel workers deserve support, echoing Jesus' own words, while also modelling the cross-shaped freedom that Philippians 2 sets out in Christ. Its athletic and racing language is taken up again in Paul's later letters and in Hebrews, where believers are urged to run with endurance the race set before them.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To defend his genuine apostleship and rights, and, more deeply, to model the self-denying love he has just commanded: a free man who surrenders real rights so that the gospel advances and many are saved, urging the Corinthians to the same disciplined, others-focused freedom.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul's specific choice to refuse support was his own strategic decision, not a universal rule; he plainly affirms that ministers may rightly be paid. What is prescriptive is the principle beneath it: love may lay down real rights for the good of others, and every believer must run and train with disciplined, persevering faith.

debates.question: Does becoming all things to all people mean compromising the truth?

views.label: Flexible in custom, fixed in the gospel
views.case: Paul adapts in matters of culture and food, becoming like a Jew to Jews and like those without the law to Gentiles, while remaining under the law of Christ; he flexes on non-essentials to remove barriers, never on the message of Christ crucified.

views.label: Mere people-pleasing
views.case: Some read it as accommodating error to win approval, but this collapses under verse 21, where Paul insists he is not outside God's law, and under his refusal elsewhere to yield an inch on the gospel.

debates.bearing: The orthodox reading frees the church for real cultural sensitivity in mission while guarding the gospel from compromise; adaptation serves the truth, it never edits it.

debates.question: What does Paul fear when he says he might be disqualified?

views.label: A warning to persevere, not a threat of lost salvation
views.case: Paul uses athletic imagery: an undisciplined runner forfeits the prize. He presses the need for self-control and endurance as the mark of true faith, in step with his confidence elsewhere that God will keep His own to the end.

views.label: A caution against empty profession
views.case: The word disqualified elsewhere describes those whose faith proves counterfeit; Paul warns that gifting and preaching without a disciplined, holy life may expose a heart that never truly belonged to Christ.

debates.bearing: Either way the verse calls for vigilant, disciplined faith rather than presumption, without teaching that a genuine believer can casually lose the salvation God secures.

study.originalLanguages: The argument turns on a single word, exousia, right or authority. Paul has every exousia to be supported, proven from soldier, farmer, Law, and temple, yet he will not wield it lest he hinder the euangelion, the gospel he is compelled to preach. The paradox sharpens at verse 19: though free from all, he uses douloo, he enslaves himself, to everyone, an echo of Christ the servant. The chapter ends in the stadium: he runs for a stephanos, a victor's crown, not the withering wreath of the games but an imperishable one, disciplining his body lest he prove adokimos, disqualified, ruled out of the prize.

typology.fulfillment: As those who served the temple and altar shared in the offerings, so under the new covenant those who preach the gospel are to live from the gospel, the shadow of Israel's priesthood realized in gospel ministry.

typology.type: The victor's wreath of the games
typology.fulfillment: The perishable crown of the athlete foreshadows the imperishable crown Christ awards to those who finish the race of faith, a fading garland answered by unfading glory.

study.christ: Christ is the pattern Paul is copying. The Lord who was rich became poor for our sake, who existed in the form of God took the form of a servant and made Himself nothing, is the model of a free Lord surrendering His rights to save. When Paul makes himself a slave to all, becomes weak with the weak, and refuses his own living, he walks the way of the cross. It is Christ who prescribed that gospel workers live from the gospel, Christ whose message Paul guards from every obstacle, and Christ who holds out the imperishable crown to those who run in faith to the end.
study.god: God stands over the whole chapter as the one who arranges and rewards. He wrote the Law that even oxen and, more so, His labourers should share in the harvest; He prescribed that preachers live from the gospel; He lays on Paul a compelling necessity so that woe would follow neglect. He is the giver of the imperishable crown and the searcher who tests each servant's work, calling His people to disciplined, persevering, self-forgetful love.

crossReferences.note: Jesus says the worker is worthy of his wages, the Lord's own prescription behind verse 14.
Christ took the form of a servant and humbled Himself, the pattern of Paul's self-surrender for others.
Paul again refuses support at Corinth so as not to burden them or hinder the gospel.
The finished race and the crown of righteousness answer the runner and the imperishable crown here.
Run with endurance the race set before us, looking to Jesus, echoing the athletic imagery of verses 24 to 27.

study.oneStory: Scripture tells of a free Lord who laid down His rights and even His life to win a people, and of a people remade to live the same way. Paul stands in that story as an imitator of Christ, surrendering real rights so the gospel runs unhindered and adapting himself to all so that some might be saved. His disciplined race for an imperishable crown points to the end of the story, when the faithful, kept by God, cross the line and receive from Christ the crown that never fades.

quiz.q: Why did Paul refuse to use his right to financial support at Corinth?

options.text: So that nothing would hinder the gospel of Christ.
options.note: Verse 12: he put up with anything rather than hinder the gospel.

options.text: So no one could accuse him of preaching for personal gain.
options.note: True and related, though Paul's stated reason is the broader one, not to hinder the gospel.

options.text: Because ministers should never be paid.
options.note: Paul affirms the right; the Lord prescribed that preachers live from the gospel.

options.text: Because he was not really an apostle.
options.note: He opens the chapter proving he is a genuine, free apostle.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by becoming all things to all people?

options.text: Flexing on custom to remove barriers, so that by all means he might save some.
options.note: He adapts culturally while remaining under the law of Christ, to win as many as possible.

options.text: Adapting his approach to different people to build a connection.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but it leaves out his aim, that some might be saved.

options.text: Adjusting the gospel message to please each audience.
options.note: He never edits the message; he adapts in non-essentials only.

options.text: Living however he pleased with no principles.
options.note: Verse 21: he is not outside God's law but under the law of Christ.

quiz.q: How does Paul establish that gospel workers deserve support?

options.text: From the soldier, farmer, and shepherd, the Law of Moses, and the temple, capped by the Lord's own command.
options.note: He piles up witnesses from life, Scripture, and Jesus Himself.

options.text: From his own authority as an apostle alone.
options.note: His apostleship matters, but he grounds the right much more widely.

options.text: From Greek customs about paying teachers.
options.note: He argues from Scripture and the Lord, not pagan custom.

options.text: He does not; he says workers deserve nothing.
options.note: He strongly affirms the right, then chooses not to use it.

quiz.q: What is the imperishable crown Paul runs for?

options.text: The unfading reward Christ gives to those who finish the race of faith.
options.note: Unlike the athlete's withering wreath, this crown never fades.

options.text: The believer's final salvation and glory with Christ.
options.note: Closely related and true, though Paul frames it as the imperishable prize won by running faithfully.

options.text: A literal wreath of leaves from the games.
options.note: That is the perishable crown he contrasts with the imperishable one.

options.text: Financial reward for his ministry.
options.note: He refused payment; the crown is the eternal prize.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by disciplining his body lest he be disqualified?

options.text: He calls for disciplined, persevering faith, as the mark of one who truly runs to win.
options.note: Like an athlete keeping the rules, he presses on with self-control to the end.

options.text: A sober warning that even gifted preachers must live holy, self-controlled lives.
options.note: A true implication, held within his call to disciplined perseverance to the end.

options.text: That a believer can casually lose salvation by one mistake.
options.note: It is a call to vigilance and endurance, not a theology of easily lost salvation.

options.text: That the body is evil and must be punished.
options.note: He teaches discipline for the race, not contempt for the body.

questions: Paul held real rights yet laid them down for the gospel. What legitimate right or freedom of yours might love ask you to set aside for someone else's good?
He became all things to all people to win them. Where could you flex on preferences and customs, without compromising the gospel, to reach people who are not like you?
Paul felt a holy compulsion, woe to me if I do not preach. What has God entrusted to you that you are tempted to treat as optional rather than as a calling?
The athlete trains with strict discipline for a fading wreath. What would disciplined, purposeful running look like in your walk with Christ this week?
Paul feared preaching to others yet being disqualified himself. How do you guard your own heart and life so that your faith is more than words?

translationNotes.note: Cephas is the Aramaic name for Peter; Paul names him alongside the other apostles and the Lord's brothers to show that travelling with a believing wife was an accepted apostolic practice.
Do not muzzle an ox is quoted from Deuteronomy 25:4; Paul reads it not as mere animal welfare but as a principle written for the sake of human workers, that the labourer should share in the harvest.
Disqualified translates adokimos, an athletic term for being ruled out of the prize; the verse warns of the need for disciplined perseverance, not that a genuine believer is easily cast away.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 9:1-27. Any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas to comply with the no-dash style rule; wording is unchanged.
